messages.author: Uncle Lit
messages.text: shutdown is not implemented in MeshCore's firmware yet. It is on the to-do list. On these devices, shutdown is a deep sleep (in meshcore or meshtastic), so battery will continue to drain. it is a useful feature for things like swapping antenna. Unfortunately it is not implemented yet.
messages.content_html: shutdown is not implemented in MeshCore's firmware yet. It is on the to-do list. On these devices, shutdown is a deep sleep (in meshcore or meshtastic), so battery will continue to drain. it is a useful feature for things like swapping antenna. Unfortunately it is not implemented yet.
messages.reply_html:
danielp
That’s what I was told. That’s unfortunate. I hope this comes in future firmware. I had to flash 2 of my doses back to Meshtastic just to power them off

messages.author: Venice FL-Ken
messages.text: @Nick D I'll check it out. I have not seen your meshcore nodes, but do see your meshtastic nodes although thru 2 hop. Only using heltec v3 but connected to yagi pointed directly at you.

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: some quick advice as you go down the meshcore rabbithole: 1) Antenna height makes a big big difference. 2) Buildings, trees, etc. really eats into the signal. Repeaters with a good view helps to make MeshCore more magical.
messages.content_html: some quick advice as you go down the meshcore rabbithole: 1) Antenna height makes a big big difference. 2) Buildings, trees, etc. really eats into the signal. Repeaters with a good view helps to make MeshCore more magical.

messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: trisweb
messages.text: Whatever waterproof project box I have lying around, and whatever mounting method makes sense for the solar panels.
messages.content_html: Whatever waterproof project box I have lying around, and whatever mounting method makes sense for the solar panels.
messages.reply_html:
Dolores
What are y'all using for solar node panels/enclosures/batteries? I plan on putting a wisblock outside. Unfortunately I won't be able to get it that high, but hopefully it helps the mesh.

messages.author: Dolores
messages.text: Great! For batteries, are you concerned about temperature? What chemistry do you use?
messages.content_html: Great! For batteries, are you concerned about temperature? What chemistry do you use?

messages.author: trisweb
messages.text: Lithium Polymer. People generally try to put them in a fireproof bag for safety and insulation, but generally the charge/discharge are so light duty that charging them in low temps tends to not be an issue.
messages.content_html: Lithium Polymer. People generally try to put them in a fireproof bag for safety and insulation, but generally the charge/discharge are so light duty that charging them in low temps tends to not be an issue.

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: The equipment for a repeater should essentially be the same as the equipment for a companion. I'm running one of each on RAK 19007s. Let me just get clear about this: You already have one (or more) companion node that you're connected to fine, right? And you want to add a repeater to your setup? (You said connection with all devices inside your house, and that sounds to me like you have multiple companion nodes already)
messages.content_html: The equipment for a repeater should essentially be the same as the equipment for a companion. I'm running one of each on RAK 19007s. Let me just get clear about this: You already have one (or more) companion node that you're connected to fine, right? And you want to add a repeater to your setup? (You said connection with all devices inside your house, and that sounds to me like you have multiple companion nodes already)
messages.reply_html:
dsg09
I was looking to set up a node, but can't get a reliable connection with all devices even inside house... Not sure if that's gonna work. Can somebody share the minimum equipment to run a repeater node?

messages.author: dsg09
messages.text: yeah, I've configured 3x T-Beam 1.2 for client nodes, and one heltec wireless tracker for room server.
messages.content_html: yeah, I've configured 3x T-Beam 1.2 for client nodes, and one heltec wireless tracker for room server.
messages.reply_html:
Splicer
The equipment for a repeater should essentially be the same as the equipment for a companion. I'm running one of each on RAK 19007s. Let me just get clear about this: You already have one (or more) companion node that you're connected to fine, right? And you want to add a repeater to your setup? (You said connection with all devices inside your house, and that sounds to me like you have multiple companion nodes already)

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: OK. And you've done the config with the repeater so that it's got the same frequencies, spreading factors, etc as your companion nodes (and preferably the other ones in your area, of course). I think you want the default USA settings and when you first flash a node I think the default is New Zealand settings.
messages.content_html: OK. And you've done the config with the repeater so that it's got the same frequencies, spreading factors, etc as your companion nodes (and preferably the other ones in your area, of course). I think you want the default USA settings and when you first flash a node I think the default is New Zealand settings.

messages.author: dsg09
messages.text: Yeah, that I've completed
messages.content_html: Yeah, that I've completed

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: Ah, so you're looking at your Contacts list with your Companions and not seeing the Repeater in your list?
messages.content_html: Ah, so you're looking at your Contacts list with your Companions and not seeing the Repeater in your list?

messages.author: dsg09
messages.text: No, I haven't even set up repeater yet. My presumption was that if I wanna reach nodes all the way in Nashua, I'm gonna need some better hardware
messages.content_html: No, I haven't even set up repeater yet. My presumption was that if I wanna reach nodes all the way in Nashua, I'm gonna need some better hardware

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: OK, I'm not sure what you mean by not reliable. Are you sending messages direct from Companion to Companion and those aren't going through?
messages.content_html: OK, I'm not sure what you mean by not reliable. Are you sending messages direct from Companion to Companion and those aren't going through?

messages.text: OK you've set up a room server and multiple companions
messages.content_html: OK you've set up a room server and multiple companions

messages.author: dsg09
messages.text: yes
messages.content_html: yes

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: As I understand it room servers work two ways: you can log in and see the messages stored, but it will also "push" messages out to individual nodes. The "push" feature I am guessing is on a scheduled basis, because the push messages will be to each specific node encoded with each node's public key. In any case they won't go out at exactly the same time.
messages.content_html: As I understand it room servers work two ways: you can log in and see the messages stored, but it will also "push" messages out to individual nodes. The "push" feature I am guessing is on a scheduled basis, because the push messages will be to each specific node encoded with each node's public key. In any case they won't go out at exactly the same time.

messages.content_html: yeah, the room server I'll figure out, it's probably something simple. My bigger question is whether I would be able to talk to remote nodes

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: And I only set up my first room server last night so I hope someone will correct any misinformation I'm spreading.
messages.content_html: And I only set up my first room server last night so I hope someone will correct any misinformation I'm spreading.

messages.author: dsg09
messages.text: woods I presume will also be an obstacle?
messages.content_html: woods I presume will also be an obstacle?

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: Yes, but not as bad as rocks. Trees will weaken your signal. Hills will stop it.
messages.content_html: Yes, but not as bad as rocks. Trees will weaken your signal. Hills will stop it.

messages.content_html: Is anybody using signal boosting to get longer reach?

messages.reply_html:
Splicer
You can be a quarter mile away but on the other side of a small hill from a repeater and never see it.

messages.author: Splicer
messages.text: One of the tools is Line of Sight where you can pick two locations (like your own and another repeater) and it will show you the landscape in between
messages.content_html: One of the tools is Line of Sight where you can pick two locations (like your own and another repeater) and it will show you the landscape in between

messages.text: Be careful with boosting because there are laws about how strong a signal you can send. Generally just having a better antenna is totally safe.

messages.author: trisweb
messages.text: Your two biggest tools are antenna and height. Even a small low power node can go 20 miles with good height and antenna. So get those right first, then think about the radio hardware. The other best tool is getting repeaters in the right place to connect in-between, which is still preferred and more reliable over increased power in one spot.
messages.content_html: Your two biggest tools are antenna and height. Even a small low power node can go 20 miles with good height and antenna. So get those right first, then think about the radio hardware. The other best tool is getting repeaters in the right place to connect in-between, which is still preferred and more reliable over increased power in one spot.
messages.reactions_html: 1
